item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm an absolute sucker for change logs like the one featured here. I had to upvote just for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you guys thought the changelog here is weird, wait till you guys see the changelog for The Sims.

Also upvoted this article mainly just for that changelog. If the original writer wants to expand on it, that Sims changelog I linked up there is a great source of inspiration…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I felt kind of so-so through the expedition notes because they didn't seem to add much except the bugbears being 'lost' in there too. I loved the 'patch notes' though and it made it all more interesting afterward. Definitely a plus to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
couple of things. this seems to be some form of simulation, of an ordinary school. which is neat. point towards plus 1. the fact they encountered what seemed to be users, is another thing towards plus one. the bugfix list is another thing to plus one. the entities, are towards a plus one.

all in all, even through all it's gramatical flaws, i enjoyed this SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really liked this. The changelog reminded me of the bizarre bug fixes for Dwarf Fortress.

Thank you for this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Music teachers should no longer hoard oboes

That's when I knew I had to upvote. Really cool payoff at the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
so awesome.

"Disciplinary actions should no longer result in fatalities." i feel like should could be capitalized here like it was earlier, but still +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is remarkably fascinating, 093-style. Well done. It also works well with its associated tale.

item.title: hooray
item.content:encoded: Starcraft references? YAY!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not gonna vote for now. My reasoning for this is because I do really like this idea. But at the same time, I feel like it feel like there's a lot of grammar issues.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Were some of the changelog entries taken straight from The Sims 3? I swear I've read them before.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I gotta ask… the bugbear things…
… the program's got 'bugs'?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I missed this somehow; that is not something I usually do and I don't know how it happened.

Anyway, despite the reference to D&D and the obvious Sims/Starcraft inspiration, I got serious OFF vibes from this and wonder if that was intentional. (Likely due to the burning plastic.)

Also, I really like that the final addendum tells us what's going on, but not how, who or, most importantly, why. You can reveal a lot while still leaving things hidden, and that really helps this article.

Also, poor bugbears. :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm pretty extremely sure this is a very unrelated coincidence

................
– hide block

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Please.

Please, turn the bugbears into a GoI.

I'll… do something you want me to do.

Just… keep writing about them…

+1 so cute

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For some reason that changelog bit made me laugh a little, probably because I was imagining all the implied glitches going on in real life crossed with Sims or Garry's Mod physics.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: that was my article, which has since been deleted. I reference it on my sandbox page's tedious commentary

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Where have I heard of these Ursine creatures before? I'm sure I read a scip about something similar recently. Something about a crashed spaceship with several deceased 'Bugbears' in it.

item.title: Feedback
item.content:encoded: I like the general Idea of the "real, yet virtual world", but at times it seems a bit too jokey, especially in the changelog; I feel this ruins the mystery a bit. Still an upvote however.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If there's enough demand (or if enough people care), I would have an addendum of them discovering the change log, and let people collab by throwing in amusing changes noted over time. I don't know how to do it policy/rule-wise though

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As soon as I hit the kid repeating his answer to the agents' questions, I thought, "Oh! This is a video game!" I wan't sure I was right, though, until the end. I was happy because electronics SCPs are one of my favorite categories but I was also a liiitle bit wary, because it's so hard to do them well. Especially the world-as-computer-program thing. But you did it!

The realization builds up slowly and the updates log is great. I caught most of the references and while I like being in on the joke, references like that in SCPs kind of breaks my immersion. So I'm caught between "keep them in! They're fun!" and "Take them out, they're too obvious and break the fourth wall a little bit." But it doesn't look like anyone else had that issue so maybe it's just me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Also, I really, really, really hope the bugbears made it back home okay.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Music teachers should no longer hoard oboes."

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting, caught my interest quite a lot and sort of expected a mention of the foundation agents at the last log. I wonder where I have heard the "Shaper" term before, I think Fifthists? Maybe. And, what came to mind for a brief while, were the ursine creatures association with SCP-1000 in my head after seeing they resided earth a long time ago.

Also bugbears.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, this is what it feels like when characters from a video game enter a beta version…
+1 of course.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Or are they…?

Or maybe they are! OR NOT?!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huge revelation: Bugbears are bugs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was kind of "meh" about this, right up until the last expedition log. The one with the updates.

That made my day.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is it bad that I came away from this being concerned for the bugbears? I hope that they managed to get out of this weird-ass school before the last update…

This is really damn good. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll try to give you a rundown when I get home, I'm posting from a phone at present.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the Ursines.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
=(

Suggestions to help/change/improve?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not going to have the chance to read through the expedition logs, but the changelog shows me that you're doing something legitimately interesting here. Unfortunately the grammar in the main description is extremely distracting. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Music teachers should no longer hoard oboes Aaaaaand upvoted. +1

Lunchroom algorithms restricted to the lunchroom; can no longer trade pudding for principal's desk. …why can't I give this +2!?

Removed miscellaneous charges from detention slips; students should no longer be accused of "securities fraud", "vicarious liability", "forum shopping", or "contempt of disbarment."

STOP TAUNTING ME, WIKI.NET! ALLOW ME TO GIVE THIS ALL THE UPVOTES!

-cries in corner-

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is awesome… I love how it portrays a programmer starting in an empty world, and building a school first. I also love the fact that it implies that there was intelligent life on earth before. Freakin' awesome. Extremely well written, too. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Another thing: The logs read like tales, not like official exploration logs. It's really draining out my enjoyment of the article. Those logs can be handled as official documents.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was referring to amount of logs but with all of the edits I have changed to an upvote. Good recovery

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In the time between my writing my comment and you writing yours, you made some edits and I changed my mind, so there you go - upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
if it's not a major issue, why no vote "for now"? O_o

EDIT: Also, I changed those words. In retrospect, you are right, they sound oddly off

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's much better now! In general, I like the idea of a real world buggy computer program. Changed to an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I, too, cannot quite bring myself to upvote. It's almost there, but there are just odd little words here and there that throw off the tone; 'glean', 'in the guise of', 'wander' all feel a bit odd. Then again, that's just me being picky - and it improves as it goes on anyway, so it's not a major issue. No vote for now.

Edit: You've got rid of 'glean', at least, and the note about music teachers hoarding oboes really amuses me for some reason, so I'll just about manage an upvote. If further improvements aren't made, however, I might retract it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I changed a bunch of stuff based on your critique. Hopefully it sounds more coherent

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually that would be pretty good. My critique above advises that partially. Sadly I posted the critique about a slightly older version of the document.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
COMMENCE COMPREHENSIVE CRITIQUE
COMPREHENSIVE CRITIQUE COMMENCED

Special Containment Procedures: Both entrances to SCP-2093 are to be locked, with at least one security guard patrolling each entryway during normal school hours. Any persons witnessed exiting SCP-2093 without outside assistance are to be detained and interrogated.

This is an extremely short Special Containment Procedures. Perhaps adding something about how the highschool has been closed and any entities attempting to enter or exit without SCPF approval should be detained? Personally, I don't see the foundation leaving a school with an SCP active.

third floor drama room of ███████ High School and located in █████████, Maryland.

This reads like there are two entrances to the spatial anomaly. Shouldn't there be a note about foundation operatives near the second entrance in the Special Containment Procedures? If there is only one entrance, you need to reformat this.

Upon entering SCP-2093 and closing both entrances, personnel inside enter the spatial anomaly, losing electronic communications with those outside.

This is just a tiny bit rough. The problem seems to stem from what the SCP is. Is the SCP a room? Or is it the spacial anomaly the room leads to? Using the second definition of SCP-2093 you could say something like: "Upon entering the drama room and closing both doors; personnel are transported into SCP-2093." You might also want to drop the part about electronic communication. It just doesn't seem to fit with the rest of the sentence.

Personnel are able to exit the room while remaining within SCP-2093,

This is what I was talking about. You need to make it clearer what SCP-2093 is. From the first sentence, I was under the impression that SCP-2093 was the drama room.

the limits of the anomaly extend apparently in all directions,

Maybe lose the apparently? This might be more of a personal preference thing.

Continued exploration has revealed the limits of the anomaly extend apparently in all directions, and attempts at leaving school property have yielded an indefinite expanse of empty grassland

I want to point out that the trouble with this sentence, and most of the rest of the SCP, is very minor. When I read the above I understand exactly what you mean but it's just a tiny bit off. In the above the problem is the word "yielding" in this sentence. Yielding implies that you are getting something like a gem or a book. But you use it to describe the results of attempting to leave. Perhaps restructuring it to say something like "The school is surrounded in an apparently endless grassland." could be better

attempt to glean as much information

Glean seems to be the wrong word here. Perhaps "obtain" or something similarly non-conversational.

Upon entering, Agent West remarks the presence of a powerful smell similar to burning plastic.

This is a problem that appears a few times in your logs. Rather than saying that they say something why don't you just have them say it? Something like:

Agent West: Do you smell that?
Agent Pierce: Yeah, kinda smells like burning plastic
(Both agents descend to the second floor)

You also do this during first contact with the ursine-entities.

Expedition 17 is where you really have to focus your editing. If I found a book that didn't open I wouldn't say "push it like a button" The dialog here desperately needs some fixing. As a first step you could completely remove the part about being unable to open the books since it adds nothing but bad dialog to the SCP.

the only problem with the changelog is that it still has the default "hide block" when you click on the collapsible. Otherwise, it's great.

To address some of the concerns others have raised about exploration logs being too much of the SCP: I think it's fine as it is. However, If you wanted to change it it would be quite easy. Instead of including the logs that show the "bug fixes" (like Expedition-018) you could have a series of addendums with time stamps that describe the "updates" and have the main description describe the school as it was when the foundation first found it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What if I removed the dialogue from entry 017? Too late, it's gone

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would have to agree with the others, this is just a tiny bit away from an upvote. The logs are formatted oddly, some of the dialog is rather stilted, and the description just doesn't work as well as it should. No vote for now. A more complete critique is coming.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's come a long way. I'm a hair's breadth from upvoting, but I think you should address the discrepancies between the description and the logs. Most notably, you say the bugbears immediately run off in the description, but the logs indicate extensive communication with them.

I don't think the dialogue within log-17 does anything, and it's odd formatting. Also, the logs are a bit overlong. Also, the Shapers/entities are never described at all, which seems an oversight.

Like the concept, needs polishing. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) No

2) In terms of length or number or content? Or just too many sentences?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
alright…I like the Idea but I have 2 issues.

1. The ursinus Are the mascot of a college in Pennsylvania is that what your trying to reference?

2. I don't usually say this but you have far too many entries and logs. A good analogy I've heard is "logs are like spice, they can improve a dish but a plate of paprika probably wont taste very good."

I think with a little editing this can definitely be improved.

not gonna vote just yet

(sorry for not getting back to you quicker. computers on the fritz.)

I was referring to the amount of logs. should of specified what i ment

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Must give thanks and credits to users Huitzil, Morrte, and Zolgamax

The first two were largely responsible for most of the better changelog entries and staying up past midnight, and the group as a whole for making it less stupid and more interesting

Prolly time for some of the stupid references I made

Spoilers!?
– hide block

- The whole thing started as a potential rewrite of SCP-664 and changed substantially since Anaxagoras claimed the rewrite first.

- Agents West and Pierce are "The Wire" references, back when there was a lot more dialogue before Expedition-017

- The "bugbears" are originally from my failed dinosaur SCP thing, old SCP-1693 "Refugees". Despite the weakness of that SCP, people loved the bugbears, called such because they are ursine creatures with insect-like faces and chitin.

- "Silos" reference from C&C Red Alert

- "Cone" is a synonym for Pylon...

- Reference to Tartessian users meant to accentuate that people from different time periods/universes were getting in. Tartessians in the continental US...?

- Garnishes and sediment were unintentional Portal references I only realized after putting them in

- Epileptic trees
